question.text: A scheme has 10 crore units outstanding. The total assets are Rs. 150 crore and total liabilities are Rs. 5 crore. If 1 crore new units are issued at the current NAV, what will be the total assets of the scheme after the new issuance?

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Current NAV = (Rs. 150 crore - Rs. 5 crore) / 10 crore = Rs. 14.50. New units issued at NAV, so money flowing in = 1 crore x Rs. 14.50 = Rs. 14.50 crore. New total assets = Rs. 150 crore + Rs. 14.50 crore = Rs. 164.50 crore. (Units outstanding become 11 crore, NAV remains Rs. 14.50.)
**The Trap:** Rs. 160.00 crore adds Rs. 10 crore (1 crore x face value Rs. 10) — confusing face value with NAV. Rs. 165.00 crore adds Rs. 15 crore (1 crore x Rs. 15, a rounded-up NAV). Rs. 155.00 crore adds only Rs. 5 crore. The key insight is that new units are issued at current NAV, not face value.
**Cross-Exam Context:** This tests the understanding that open-ended fund units are issued at NAV, not face value. New issuances at NAV ensure existing unitholders' interests are not diluted. This is a concept tested in NISM V-A and V-B.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When clients ask why they get fewer units when investing in a scheme with high NAV, explain that units are allotted at current NAV. A higher NAV means more value per unit. The total value of investment remains the same regardless of number of units — what matters is the percentage growth of NAV over time.
